title: SRCNN: The First Deep Learning Approach
concept: SRCNN was one of the first CNNs applied to super-resolution: a simple three-layer network trained end-to-end to map low-resolution image patches directly to high-resolution ones, minimizing pixel-wise error.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main risk of treating a Super-Resolution output as verified fact?

options.text: It's a plausible reconstruction, not guaranteed to match reality exactly
SR only works on video, not still images
SR always reduces image resolution instead of increasing it

exercises.explain: Yes! SR output is a plausible guess, not verified truth.

cards.title: A humble three-layer start
cards.body: Before today's complex generative SR models, the very first deep learning attempt at super-resolution was surprisingly simple: just three convolutional layers, trained to directly map blurry patches to sharp ones.

cards.title: SRCNN
cards.body: SRCNN (Super-Resolution CNN) is an early, simple CNN architecture: it takes an upscaled-but-blurry image (via basic interpolation) and passes it through 3 conv layers trained to predict the sharp, high-resolution version, minimizing pixel-wise (L2/MSE) error against ground truth.

cards.title: Tracing over a faint sketch
cards.body: SRCNN is like tracing over a faint pencil sketch with a dark pen — the rough shape (interpolated blurry image) is already there; the network's job is just to trace clean, sharp lines on top of it.

cards.title: A three-step pipeline
cards.body: Input: a 32x32 image bicubically upscaled to 128x128 (still blurry). SRCNN's 3 conv layers process this blurry 128x128 image and output a sharper 128x128 image, trained to match the true high-res photo pixel-for-pixel.

cards.title: Simple ≠ useless
cards.body: It's tempting to dismiss SRCNN as too simple to matter. In fact, its simplicity was the point — it proved that a small, end-to-end trainable CNN could outperform decades of hand-crafted interpolation algorithms, kicking off the entire deep learning SR field.

left.text:
Fixed mathematical rule (bicubic)
No learning from data
Blurry, generic upscaling

right.text:
Learned from millions of image pairs
3-layer CNN, trained end-to-end
Sharper, learned reconstruction

exercises.prompt: What loss does SRCNN minimize during training?

options.text: Pixel-wise (L2/MSE) error between predicted and true high-resolution images
The number of bounding boxes detected

exercises.explain: Correct! SRCNN directly minimizes pixel-wise reconstruction error.

exercises.prompt: True or false: SRCNN starts from raw low-resolution pixels with no initial upscaling step.
exercises.explain: False. SRCNN first applies basic (bicubic) interpolation to upscale the image, then refines it with conv layers.

exercises.sentence: SRCNN proved that a small, end-to-end trainable CNN could outperform decades of hand-crafted ___ algorithms.
exercises.explain: SRCNN outperformed classical interpolation methods like bicubic upscaling.

exercises.prompt: Why is SRCNN historically significant despite being architecturally simple?

options.text: It proved learned features could beat classical interpolation, launching the deep learning SR field
It was the first model to use skip connections
It eliminated the need for any training data

exercises.explain: Correct! Its simplicity proved the core deep-learning-for-SR concept.

exercises.prompt: Why might a pixel-wise (MSE) loss like SRCNN's produce outputs that look slightly blurry compared to real photos, even when trained well?

options.text: MSE rewards 'safe' average predictions across many plausible textures, which tends to blur fine, sharp detail
MSE loss makes the network output random noise instead of images
MSE loss only works for classification tasks, not image outputs
There is no such effect; MSE-trained SR always looks perfectly sharp

exercises.explain: Exactly! Averaging over plausible outputs blurs fine texture — this motivates the next lesson: perceptual loss.
